article.title: FAA Launches eVTOL Pilot Program Across 26 States as Electric Aviation Enters Decisive Year
article.summary: The FAA's new eVTOL pilot program will allow Joby, Archer, Beta, and Wisk to fly across 26 states this summer, while RTX hits a full-power milestone on its hybrid-electric regional aircraft demonstrator.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Electric aviation reached an inflection point in March 2026, with the U.S. federal government formally selecting eight proposals under a new pilot program that will allow electric vertical takeoff and landing aircraft to fly across 26 states, while a parallel effort in hybrid-electric propulsion hit a milestone that could reshape regional air travel. Taken together, the developments mark the beginning of a year that will determine whether the electric aviation sector can convert a decade of prototyping and fundraising into operational aircraft carrying passengers and cargo.

## The eVTOL Integration Pilot Program

On March 9, the White House-backed eVTOL Integration Pilot Program selected eight proposals that will give four companies — Joby Aviation, Archer Aviation, Beta Technologies, and Wisk — the authority to begin pre-certification operations as early as this summer, [according to TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/09/electric-air-taxis-are-about-to-take-flight-in-26-states/). The three-year program, established by presidential executive order, is designed to accelerate regulatory coordination between federal and local authorities while generating operational data that the FAA can use to refine its certification framework.

Joby Aviation secured the broadest geographic footprint among the selectees, winning inclusion in multiple proposals that will allow operations in Arizona, Florida, Idaho, New Jersey, New York, North Carolina, Oklahoma, Oregon, Texas, and Utah. CEO JoeBen Bevirt said in a company statement that "communities across America are going to see it in the skies above their own cities this year." The company has begun flight testing its first FAA-conforming aircraft for Type Inspection Authorization, with FAA pilots expected to visit Joby's facility in Marina, California, later this year to conduct validation testing.

Archer Aviation will operate through partnerships with the Texas Department of Transportation, the Florida Department of Transportation, and the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. In a notable certification milestone, Archer became the first company to achieve 100 percent FAA acceptance of its eVTOL Means of Compliance — the criteria the agency uses to determine whether an aircraft meets airworthiness requirements. The company's second piloted Midnight aircraft received its special airworthiness certificate in February 2026.

Beta Technologies was included in seven of the eight selected proposals, giving it presence in at least 10 states. The company has [surpassed 100,000 nautical miles of flight testing](https://www.flightglobal.com/aerospace/busy-beta-builds-on-100000nm-test-milestone-as-real-world-flights-pay-dividends/165846.article) with its ALIA aircraft, a milestone that CEO Kyle Clark said has offered critical lessons for the certification push. Beta is pursuing a phased approach: its H500A electric engine is on track for FAA type certification in the first half of 2026, followed by the CX300 conventional takeoff variant by late 2026 or early 2027, and the eVTOL A250 roughly a year after that.

Operations under the pilot program are expected to begin within 90 days of contracts being finalized, with use cases spanning passenger transport, cargo logistics, and emergency medical response.

## RTX Hybrid-Electric Demonstrator Reaches Full Power

While the eVTOL sector focuses on short-range urban air mobility, RTX is pursuing electric propulsion at a larger scale. On March 3, the company announced that its Hybrid-Electric Flight Demonstrator successfully operated its integrated propulsion system and batteries at full power for the first time in a test cell at Pratt & Whitney Canada's facility near Montreal, [according to RTX](https://www.rtx.com/news/2026/03/03/rtxs-hybrid-electric-plane-is-one-step-closer-to-the-sky).

The demonstrator pairs a derivative of Pratt & Whitney's PW127XT turboprop engine with a 1-megawatt electric motor built by Collins Aerospace and a 200-kilowatt-hour battery pack developed from architecture provided by Swiss startup H55, [as detailed in a press release distributed by PR Newswire](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/h55-battery-technology-helps-power-progress-of-rtx-hybrid-electric-flight-demonstrator-302717460.html). A specialized gearbox allows both power sources to drive the propeller shaft simultaneously or independently, depending on the flight phase: the electric motor handles taxi, takeoff, and climb, while the thermal engine manages cruise.

RTX projects the architecture can deliver up to a 30 percent improvement in fuel efficiency compared to today's most advanced regional turboprops, a figure that reflects the fundamental efficiency advantage of electric motors, which convert more than 90 percent of energy to mechanical power versus 30 to 40 percent for combustion engines. Ground testing will continue through 2026, with flight testing planned at AeroTEC in Moses Lake, Washington, using a modified De Havilland Canada Dash 8-100.

The hybrid-electric approach targets a different market segment than eVTOLs. Rather than replacing cars on short urban trips, it aims to reduce fuel consumption on existing regional routes — a category that accounts for a substantial share of commercial aviation emissions. If the demonstrator meets its targets, the technology could be applied as a retrofit to turboprop fleets already in service.

## The Gap Between Certification and Commercial Viability

Despite the regulatory momentum, significant questions remain about whether the economics of electric aviation can support the commercial timelines the industry has set. A [February analysis published by CleanTechnica](https://cleantechnica.com/2026/02/13/evtol-certification-is-coming-but-commercial-runway-isnt/) noted that certification probability for any single eVTOL company reaching type certification by 2027 sits at roughly 20 to 30 percent, with 2028 considered more realistic.

The financial pressure is substantial. Archer's operating expenses rose 43 percent year-over-year to $174.8 million in the third quarter of 2025, a trajectory typical of late-stage aerospace development programs. Under scenarios where burn rates double during final certification — a common pattern in novel aircraft programs — most firms face funding pressure by 2027, even with current liquidity positions that range from approximately $1.6 billion for Archer and $1.55 billion for Joby to roughly $1.7 billion for Beta.

Engineering complexity compounds the financial challenge. Distributed electric propulsion systems, fly-by-wire controls for novel aircraft configurations, and new safety objectives for a category of vehicle that has never existed at commercial scale create what the CleanTechnica analysis described as "unpredictable timelines." Rotorwash hazards — the hurricane-force winds generated near the aircraft during vertical flight — constrain vertiport design and reduce the number of viable urban landing sites, directly affecting the route networks that underpin revenue projections.

Breaking even may require roughly 2,500 aircraft sales at an estimated $3 million per unit with 20 percent margins, yet market assessments suggest demand in the low thousands globally over a decade rather than annually.

## What Comes Next

The eIPP represents a deliberate bet by the federal government that controlled, pre-certification operations will generate the safety data and public familiarity needed to bring electric aircraft into routine service. For the companies involved, the next 18 months will be defined by parallel races: completing the engineering work to satisfy FAA type certification requirements while demonstrating to investors that commercial viability exists beyond the certification milestone.

RTX's hybrid-electric work operates on a longer timeline but addresses a larger addressable market. Regional turboprops fly millions of passenger-miles annually on routes too short for jets but too long for cars. A 30 percent fuel savings on those routes, achieved through a retrofit rather than an entirely new aircraft, would bypass many of the infrastructure and regulatory challenges facing eVTOLs.

The overarching question for 2026 is whether the industry can maintain its current trajectory. Cash reserves are finite, certification timelines are uncertain, and the infrastructure needed for commercial eVTOL operations — vertiports, charging networks, air traffic management systems — remains largely unbuilt. The pilot program buys time, but not indefinitely.
